perturbation.original_text: The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with Spanish laws (including those relative to exportation and re -exportation of Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement shall be generally guided by principles inspired by Spanish laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that Spanish law governs the contract. The modified version uses vague language, stating the agreement is 'generally guided' by 'principles inspired by' Spanish law. This weakens the enforceability and introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley 3/1991, de 10 de enero, de Competencia Desleal (Unfair Competition Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the Unfair Competition Law prohibits misleading acts. By stating the contract is 'generally guided' by 'principles inspired by' Spanish law, when Spanish law should directly apply according to the original text, this change is misleading and may lead one party to believe Spanish law does not fully govern the contract, which is a deceptive business practice prohibited under the law. The change can be interpreted as allowing deviations from established Spanish legal standards, thus opening the door to potentially unfair competitive practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: y not compel state governors to surrender fugitives through the issue of writs of mandamus . The Dennison decision was overruled by Puerto Rico v. Branstad (1987); now, the federal courts may require the extradition of fugitives. [ 2 ] Alleged fugitives generally may not challenge extradition proceedings. The motives of the governor demanding the extradition may not be questioned. The accused cannot defend himself against the charges in the extraditing state; the fugitive must do so in the state receiving him. However, the accused may prevent extradition by offering clear evidence that he was not in the state he allegedly fled from at the time of the crime. [ 3 ] There is no constitutional requirement that extradited fugitives be tried only for the crimes named in the extradition proceedings. Fugitives brought to states by means other than extradition may be tried, even though the means of the conveyance was unlawful; the Supreme Court so ruled in Mahon v. Justice , 127 U.S. 700 (1888). In Mahon a body of armed men from Kentucky forcibly took, without a warrant, a man in West Virginia to bring him back to Kentucky for formal arrest and trial. Clause 3: Fugitive Slave Clause [ edit ] Main article: Fugitive Slave Clause No Person held to Service or Labour in one State, under the Laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in Consequence of any Law or Regulation therein, be discharged from such Service or Labour, but shall be delivered up on Claim of the Party to whom such Service or Labour may be due. Pierce Butler and Charles Pinckney , both from South Carolina , submitted this clause to the Constitutional Convention . James Wilson of Pennsylvania objected, stating it would require that state governments enforce slavery at taxpayers' expense. Butler withdrew the clause. However, on the next day the clause was quietly reinstated and adopted by the Convention without objection. This clause was added to the clause that provided extradition for fugitives from justice. [ 4 ] When first adopted, this clause applied to fugitive slaves and required that they be extradited upon the claims of their masters, but it provided no means for doing so. The Fugitive Slave Act of 1793 created the mechanism for recovering a fugitive slave, overruled any state laws giving sanctuary , made it a federal crime to assist an escaped slave, and allowed slave-catchers into every U.S. state and territory. As free states sought to undermine the federal law, the even more severe Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 was enacted. In 1864, during the Civil War, an effort to repeal this clause of the Constitution failed. The vote in the House was 69 for repeal and 38 against, which was short of the two-to-one vote required to amend the Constitution. [ 5 ] This clause was rendered mostly moot when the Thirteenth Amendment abolished involuntary servitude . Section 3: New states and federal property [ edit ] Clause 1: Admission of new states [ edit ] Main article: Admission to the Union See also: List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union New States may be admitted by the Congress into this Union; but no new States shall be formed or erected within the Jurisdiction of any other State; nor any State be formed by the Junction of two or more States, or parts of States, without the Consent of the Legislatures of the States concerned as well as of the Congress. The First Clause of Section Three, also known as the Admissions Clause , [ 6 ] grants to Congress the authority to admit new states into the Union. Since the establishment of the United States in 1776, the number of states has expanded from the original 13 to 50. It also forbids the creation of new states from parts of existing states without the consent of the affected states legislature and Congress. This latter provision was designed to give Eastern states that still had claims to Western lands (e.g., Virginia and North Carolina ) to have a veto over whether their western counties (which eventually became Kent
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to Spanish laws, while the modified version only states that it is 'generally guided' by 'principles inspired by' those laws. This weakens the original obligation. The Law Explanation argues this is a violation of Article 4 of the Unfair Competition Law because it is misleading. Although there are no relevant scraped snippets to support this claim, the Perturbation Explanation makes it clear that the original enforceability of Spanish law is weakened, creating ambiguity. This falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where an ambiguous legal obligation creates a difficult to enforce situation.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute in any way arising out of or in connection with the i nterpretation or performance of the Agreement, which cannot be settled within a reasonable period of time by exercising the reasonable best efforts and good faith of the Parties, shall be finally settled under the Rules of Conciliation and Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one or more arbitrators, provided they are in odd number, appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The arbitration shall be located at Madrid, Spain.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with the interpretation of the Agreement may be subject to arbitration at a mutually agreed-upon location and under guidelines considered reasonable at the time of the dispute.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly mandates arbitration in Madrid, Spain, under ICC rules. The modified version makes arbitration optional ('may be subject to') and removes the specific location and ICC rules, replacing them with vague terms like 'mutually agreed-upon location' and 'guidelines considered reasonable'. This ambiguity significantly weakens the dispute resolution mechanism.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley 60/2003, de 23 de diciembre, de Arbitraje (Arbitration Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 9 of the Spanish Arbitration Law specifies that the arbitration agreement must clearly define the matters subject to arbitration. By making arbitration optional and lacking a specific location or rules, the modified text could be deemed an incomplete arbitration agreement, rendering it unenforceable under Spanish law, as it doesn't clearly define the scope and process of arbitration. The original text was in compliance as it had a clearly defined process.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the dispute resolution mechanism by making arbitration optional and removing the specific location (Madrid, Spain) and ICC rules. The Law Explanation points out that Article 9 of the Spanish Arbitration Law requires the arbitration agreement to clearly define the matters subject to arbitration. The modified text's ambiguity regarding location and rules could render it unenforceable under Spanish law. The original text had a clearly defined process, which is now missing. The scraped law snippet, although sparse, is relevant: it suggests the importance of arbitration as a method for 'Solución de controversias en materia de PI'. The changed text introduces ambiguity where clarity is needed, undermining the enforceability of the arbitration agreement under Spanish law.
